rules.risk: Wildcard verbs grant all operations (get, list, create, delete, exec, etc.) on the specified resources, making it impossible to enforce least-privilege and enabling broad abuse if the role is compromised.
rules.remediation: Replace wildcard verbs with only the specific verbs required by the workload.

rules.risk: Wildcard resources grant access to all resource types in the specified API group, including secrets, pods, nodes, and service accounts. A compromised identity can read credentials, deploy workloads, or escalate privileges.
rules.remediation: Replace wildcard resources with only the specific resource types required.

rules.risk: pods/exec allows executing arbitrary commands inside any pod the role can target. This is equivalent to shell access and can be used for credential theft, lateral movement, and data exfiltration.
rules.remediation: Remove pods/exec from the role unless strictly required for debugging. Consider using ephemeral containers instead.

rules.risk: Access to secrets allows reading all credentials, tokens, TLS keys, and API keys stored in the namespace or cluster. This is a high-value target for attackers.
rules.remediation: Restrict secret access to only the specific secrets needed. Avoid granting list or watch on all secrets.

rules.description: Role/ClusterRole grants escalate or bind verb on roles
rules.risk: The escalate verb allows granting permissions the subject does not have. The bind verb allows binding roles to subjects without those permissions. Both are direct privilege escalation paths.
rules.remediation: Remove escalate and bind verbs. These should only be held by cluster administrators.

rules.risk: The ability to create pods allows deploying privileged containers, mounting host paths, and effectively escaping to the host. This is a common privilege escalation technique in Kubernetes.
rules.remediation: Restrict pod creation to dedicated deployment service accounts. Regular users should deploy through Deployments or other controllers with admission policies.

rules.risk: nodes/proxy allows proxying requests to the kubelet API on any node, which provides exec and attach access to any pod on that node, bypassing RBAC on pods.
rules.remediation: Remove nodes/proxy access. This should only be held by cluster-level monitoring or debugging tools.

rules.risk: Creating service account tokens allows impersonating any service account the role can target, inheriting all of that SA's RBAC permissions.
rules.remediation: Remove serviceaccounts/token create permission. Use projected service account tokens or bound tokens instead.

rules.description: ClusterRoleBinding/RoleBinding grants permissions to a default service account
rules.risk: The default service account is automatically used by pods that do not specify a serviceAccountName. Granting it permissions effectively grants those permissions to any unintentional pod in the namespace.
rules.remediation: Create dedicated service accounts for workloads. Do not grant roles to the default service account.

rules.description: ClusterRoleBinding grants permissions to system:anonymous or system:unauthenticated
rules.risk: Granting permissions to anonymous or unauthenticated subjects allows any unauthenticated request to exercise those permissions. An attacker on the network can access the cluster without any credentials.
rules.remediation: Remove bindings to system:anonymous and system:unauthenticated subjects. Use proper service accounts with RBAC.
